description: Fix browser_execute_script silently dropping async/await results. The inner function wrapper is non-async, causing await expressions to produce a syntax error that chrome.scripting.executeScript swallows silently, resulting in 'No result captured' instead of the actual value.

userStories.title: Make execute_script wrapper async to support await expressions
userStories.description: As a developer using browser_execute_script, I want await expressions in my code to work correctly so that I can use fetch() and other async APIs without falling back to synchronous XMLHttpRequest.
acceptanceCriteria: The inner function wrapper in writeExecFile uses 'async function' instead of 'function'
Code with await expressions returns the correct value (e.g., 'return await Promise.resolve(42)' returns 42)
Code with await fetch() returns the fetched data correctly
Code with multiple await expressions works (sequential awaits in one script)
Synchronous code continues to work correctly (return document.title, return 1+1, etc.)
Async rejections are captured as errors (return await Promise.reject('fail') returns {error: 'fail'})
Thrown errors inside async code are captured (await something; throw new Error('boom') returns {error: 'boom'})
The existing 10-second async timeout still applies to long-running async operations
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/adapter-files.ts, line 183.

The root cause: the user's code is wrapped in a non-async IIFE:
  var __r = (function() { <user code> })();

When user code contains 'await', this is a syntax error (await is only valid in async functions). The .js file fails to parse entirely, so chrome.scripting.executeScript silently succeeds with undefined result. The polling loop in extension-commands.ts:450 finds neither __execResult_ nor __execAsync_ keys and returns {error: 'No result captured'}.

The fix is a one-word change on line 183:
  var __r = (async function() { <user code> })();

Since async function always returns a Promise, the thenable detection on line 186 will always trigger. The existing .then()/.catch() handlers and polling loop already handle Promises correctly.

Tradeoff: synchronous code like 'return document.title' will now return a Promise (wrapped by async function), adding one extra polling cycle (~50ms). This is negligible and the tool description already claims async support.

The 'No result captured' error at extension-commands.ts:450 is the symptom, not the cause — do NOT change that code. The fix is entirely in adapter-files.ts.

Check the writeExecFile function carefully — the line numbers may have shifted. Search for '(function()' in the exec file template to find the exact location.

userStories.title: Add E2E tests for async browser_execute_script
userStories.description: As a platform maintainer, I want E2E test coverage for async code execution in browser_execute_script so that async support is verified and regressions are caught.
acceptanceCriteria: E2E test: 'return await Promise.resolve("async-value")' returns {value: 'async-value'}
E2E test: multiple sequential awaits work ('const a = await Promise.resolve(1); const b = await Promise.resolve(2); return a + b' returns {value: 3})
E2E test: await fetch() works (fetches the current page URL and returns the HTTP status code 200)
E2E test: async rejection is captured ('return await Promise.reject("async-fail")' returns an error containing 'async-fail')
E2E test: sync code still works as a regression check ('return document.title' returns the page title)
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
npm run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
E2E tests go in the e2e/ directory. Look at existing browser_execute_script tests to follow the same pattern — there should be existing tests in e2e/tests/ that exercise browser tools.

The E2E test framework uses Playwright and the MCP server. Look at how other browser tool tests call browser_execute_script and check the result.

The test tab should be the e2e-test tab (localhost:6006 or similar test server) that's already used in existing E2E tests. Use whatever tab setup pattern the existing tests use.

Key test cases:
1. Basic await: return await Promise.resolve('value')
2. Multiple awaits: const a = await ...; const b = await ...; return a + b
3. await fetch: const r = await fetch(window.location.href); return r.status
4. Async rejection: return await Promise.reject('fail') — should be an error
5. Regression: return document.title — sync still works

Do NOT test the 10-second timeout in E2E (too slow). That can be a unit test.
